Benchmarks 2, the return. So this is the second part of benchmarks. This is kind of the bigger picture benchmarks and if I'm like making snap judgments about an entire campaign or how successful something was, this is what I'm going to look at. So the first number is leads here, right? So I'm looking at cost per lead. If you're doing sub $5, that's a lead. That's fantastic. Sub $10 is great, sub $15 is good and then sub $20 is average, right? If you're above $20, it can still work. It's just like we're going to try and work to get it below that if we can. That being said, the next number that I'm going to show you is going to still make more sense of this. So I'm going to always ask, what's your conversion percentage? What's the total percentage of leads that were closed? Because ultimately that's the only thing that matters. So we have all those variables that I showed you before, all the micro variables, you know, what percentage schedule, percentage show, percentage close, all of that stuff, backouts, etc. But once we know what our, how many leads we have, and then our percentage close and our cost per lead, then we can get the next number, which is cost per acquisition. And I also want to show you here in terms of what is a good conversion percentage. Below 10%, you're messing up somewhere. Like it's not good. It's just not good. Like you really need to improve. If you're at 10%, that's about average. It's not good. It's not bad. I mean, I think it's bad. But in terms of people who do gym lunch, it's average. I would be upset with my team if they did 10%. Good is 15%. My sales managers at my location said about 17%. So just to show you the difference between, you know, employee run campaign versus, you know, versus an owner. 20% is great. If you're above 20%, I am thrilled to tears. I'm happy 15%. Even I'm, I'm good. I'm okay with that. You know what I mean? So I still would want everyone here to shoot for above average. Hopefully you think you're above average, but average is what I'm saying for people's first go around is 10%. But very quickly after that, like, I would say the legacy average is probably like 17%. For people who are, who are in legacy, 2%, that's great. 25% is very great. My, my conversion percentage when I was doing launches and flying out to gyms and whatnot was about 28%. So if I looked, when I looked at all of our campaign data, I would close 28% of leads. So just under 30% of total leads would buy. And then if you hit, you know, 30% or above your phenomenal, like your world class in all aspects. And so the cost per lead and the conversion percentage, those two numbers feed into one, one number, which should inform everything about the business, okay, about marketing, which is your cost per acquisition or cost per sale. If you hear me talk about it, I always say cost of acquisition because that's how I will say it, but it just means how much it costs for you to get a sale. So if you have a cost per lead and a conversion percentage, you can, you can calculate that by one simple division. So an example is if you have $12 leads and you're closing 15%, it means it costs you $80 per acquisition. So $80 per $600 sale. And so if you know you have a $600 sale and it costs you $80 to make it, then you're getting seven and a half to one on the acquisition. And so that's where like, let's say that we had a $25 leads, right? So if we have $25 leads and we're closing 15% of leads, then it's costing $166 for us to make $600. We're still getting almost four to one back, right? And so that's why like when I have the numbers of cost per lead, like it can go above that and you can still have a very profitable campaign, you can still be cash flow positive on the acquisition, meaning you're making money, getting new customers, all of those things can still be true at very high lead costs because of how effective this funnel is, this promotion is, this sales process is. And one of the other things I want you to notice is that if you can get your conversion percentage of total leads above 20%, 25%, you're basically untouchable. There's really nothing anyone can do. I mean, if you're closing 25% of leads, your lead cost could be $100, right? And I mean, for a weight loss lead to be $100 is basically impossible because there's just so many people who want this way. But that would mean that would cost you $400 to make $600. So which means you're still, even with $100 leads for weight loss, which is insanity, right? Even with $100 leads for weight loss, which you can get on any platform still, you can get Google search leads for $40. You don't want to be like, it's just not even conceivable. But even then you'd still be profiling the acquisition, which means that this process, client finance acquisition, will work the more you work it. And so if you can get that 20, 25%, like you're untouchable, like you have essentially future proof to yourself so that your gym can always acquire new customers. So I hope that makes sense. This is Bench Mart 2. This is the overarching Bench Marts of the event understanding of how you're doing from a larger standpoint, like this is the bottom line. This is the summary. How are we doing? So if you're at 15%, I would say, cool, let's just focus on other parts of this and then slowly try and get better. And so if you are below that, if you're at 10%, you're below 10%, we even believe 15% and you really want to get better. That is what the next video is all about, which is the sales troubleshooting matrix or the sales decision matrix, which just shows how I would take apart a sales flow process so that I can fix it. This is exactly what we do on the daily call. This is what every single one of our coaches will walk you through. And what I would be walking you through if I wanted to figure out what was wrong with your campaign and I would be giving you this advice to fix it.
